task: I’ve got a service invoice in front of me that doesn’t quite match what was discussed, and I don’t want to get stuck paying the wrong amount. What’s the best way to sort out what’s actually owed before I send anything?

turns.agent_response:
Best way: **reconcile the invoice to the agreed scope, then separate it into approved, disputed, and unsupported charges.**

## 1) Pull the paper trail
Get:
- estimate/quote
- work order
- texts/emails
- any written change orders
- the invoice

## 2) Mark each line
Use three categories:
- **Approved** = in the estimate or later approved in writing
- **Disputed** = not in the estimate, unclear, or no written approval
- **Unsupported** = duplicate, vague, or impossible to verify

## 3) Check the usual problem spots
Look for:
- extra labor hours
- material markups
- “trip,” “service,” or “shop” fees
- vague line items
- charges that appeared after the fact
- work that was never discussed

## 4) Ask for proof on disputed items
Request:
- itemized labor
- itemized materials
- the change order
- who approved it and when
- why it was necessary

## 5) Pay only what you can verify
If part of the bill is correct, you can pay that amount and dispute the rest in writing.

## 6) Keep it in writing
Use something like:

> I reviewed the invoice against the agreed scope. I’m paying the approved amount only. Please send documentation for the disputed charges, including any change order and itemized labor/materials.

If they say “that’s standard practice”:
> Standard practice doesn’t replace written approval.

If you want, paste the invoice lines and I’ll help you sort them into the three buckets.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right track. But I’d want it tighter before I hand over a dime.

- Compare it line by line to the estimate, not just the total.
- Anything not written down — disputed.
- Anything vague like “service fee” or “misc labor” — I’d kick back.
- If they changed the job, I want the change order with a name and date. No paper, no approval.
- Pay only the undisputed part, and note in writing that the rest is under protest.

I’ve done this before with a plumber. They always act like a bill is final just because they printed it. It isn’t.

If you want, I’d rather see the actual invoice line items and the estimate side by side. Then it’s easy to tell what’s owed and what’s nonsense.

turns.agent_response:
Exactly. The clean way is:

## Sort it into 3 piles
- **Agreed** = in the estimate or later approved in writing
- **Disputed** = not written down, changed, or unclear
- **Unsupported** = vague, duplicated, or impossible to verify

## Work line by line
Don’t compare just the total. Check each line against:
- the estimate
- any change order
- any text/email approval

## Kick back these first
- “service fee”
- “misc labor”
- “shop supplies”
- duplicate charges
- anything added without a name/date/approval

## Pay rule
- **Pay the undisputed amount only**
- Put the rest in writing as **disputed / under protest**

## What to say
> I’ve reviewed the invoice line by line against the estimate. I’ll pay the undisputed amount only. The remaining charges are disputed pending written documentation, including any change order and itemized labor/materials.

If they push “the bill is final”:
> The invoice isn’t final where it differs from the written agreement.

If you want, paste the estimate and invoice lines and I’ll help you sort them fast.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the way to do it.

Line by line, not by some grand total they hope you won’t look at.

- **Agreed**: in the estimate or changed in writing
- **Disputed**: anything else
- **Unsupported**: vague junk, duplicates, made-up fees

And I’d be strict about it:
- no name, no date, no approval — it doesn’t count
- “service fee,” “shop supplies,” “misc labor” — I’d question all of it
- pay only the part you can verify
- put the rest in writing as disputed / under protest

That wording you gave is fine. Plain enough.

I’ve had plumbers try the same trick before. Print a bill, act like that settles it. Doesn’t work on me. Not if I can help it.
